questions.question: What are the four primitives that span the entire multi-agent design space?
options: Model, prompt, dataset, evaluator
Agent (system prompt + tools), handoff (structured control transfer), shared state, and orchestrator (who decides who speaks next)
Client, server, gateway, registry
Plan, act, observe, reflect
questions.explanation: Every shipping framework — AutoGen, LangGraph, CrewAI, OpenAI Agents SDK, Microsoft Agent Framework — is a point in this four-dimensional space. Learn the axes once and read any new release in a paragraph.

questions.question: Why does learning frameworks one at a time burn you out, per this lesson?
options: The docs are all in different languages
Surface syntax differs wildly (blackboard vs message pool vs StateGraph) while the underlying primitives are stable — the churn is naming, not substance
Each framework requires its own GPU
Frameworks expire every six months
questions.explanation: AutoGen 2023, CrewAI 2024, LangGraph/Swarm 2024, Google ADK 2025, Microsoft Agent Framework 2026 — each picks defaults on the same four axes. The comparison table is the whole story.

questions.question: Which three checklist questions answer ~80% of framework selection?
options: Price, license, GitHub stars
Does the orchestrator trust the LLM to route or pin routing in code? Is shared state full-history or projected? Can agents modify each other's prompts or only hand off?
Python or TypeScript? Cloud or local? Open or closed?
Token limit, latency, context window
questions.explanation: Swarm trusts the LLM's next handoff call; LangGraph pins routing in a deterministic graph. GroupChat carries full history; StateGraph reduces. Those axes — not the marketing — decide fit.

questions.question: What is the "stateless insight," and where do the interesting bugs live?
options: Everything in a multi-agent system is stateful
Agent, handoff, and orchestrator are all stateless functions — shared state is the only stateful thing, so memory poisoning, ordering, versioning, and write contention all live there
Bugs are evenly distributed across primitives
Stateless systems cannot have bugs
questions.explanation: An agent is a function of (prompt, tools); two agents with the same prompt and tools are interchangeable. Frameworks that hide shared state (Swarm) push the problem to the caller; those that centralize it (LangGraph checkpoints) make it inspectable.

questions.question: Mechanically, what IS a handoff in most 2026 frameworks?
options: A network socket transfer between machines
A tool call that returns a new agent, or a graph edge that follows a condition — a structured (from, to, reason, payload) transfer of control
Copying one model's weights into another
An email between agent owners
questions.explanation: OpenAI Swarm implements handoff as a tool returning an Agent; LangGraph as a conditional edge; CrewAI as a process ordering. Same primitive, three syntaxes.
